concepts.lesson: Heredity is the transmission of characteristics from parents to their offspring through genes. It explains why children resemble their parents in many traits.
concepts.real_example: A child inheriting eye colour or hair type similar to their parents.
key_points: Traits pass from parents to offspring

concepts.lesson: Variation refers to the differences in characteristics among individuals of the same species. These differences arise due to genetic recombination and environmental factors.
concepts.real_example: Siblings in the same family may have different heights or hair textures.

concepts.lesson: Genes are units of heredity made of DNA and located on chromosomes. They carry instructions for specific traits and pass from parents to offspring during reproduction.
concepts.real_example: A gene controlling blood group determines whether a person has A, B, AB, or O blood type.

concepts.lesson: Genotype refers to the genetic makeup of an organism, while phenotype refers to the observable physical characteristics that result from the genotype.
concepts.real_example: A plant with genotype Tt appears tall because the dominant gene expresses the tall trait.
key_points: Phenotype depends on genotype and environment

concepts.lesson: Gregor Mendel conducted experiments on pea plants to study inheritance. By crossing plants with contrasting traits, he discovered patterns in how traits are inherited.
concepts.real_example: Crossing tall pea plants with dwarf plants to observe the traits in the next generations.

concepts.lesson: Mendel's law of dominance states that when two different alleles are present, only the dominant allele is expressed in the offspring.
concepts.real_example: In pea plants, tallness (T) is dominant over dwarfness (t), so Tt plants appear tall.
key_points: Dominant trait expresses in heterozygous condition

concepts.lesson: A monohybrid cross studies the inheritance of a single trait. It helps determine how dominant and recessive traits appear in different generations.
concepts.real_example: Cross between tall (TT) and dwarf (tt) pea plants producing tall plants in F1 generation.
key_points: Involves one pair of contrasting traits

concepts.lesson: A dihybrid cross studies the inheritance of two traits simultaneously. It demonstrates Mendel's law of independent assortment.
concepts.real_example: Cross between pea plants differing in seed shape and seed colour producing a 9:3:3:1 ratio in the F2 generation.

concepts.lesson: In humans, sex is determined by sex chromosomes. Females have XX chromosomes, while males have XY chromosomes. The chromosome carried by the sperm determines the sex of the child.
concepts.real_example: If an X chromosome sperm fertilizes the egg, the child will be female; if a Y chromosome sperm fertilizes it, the child will be male.
key_points: Sperm determines the sex of the offspring

concepts.lesson: Inherited traits are passed through genes from parents to offspring, while acquired traits develop during an organism's lifetime due to environmental influences and are not passed to the next generation.
concepts.real_example: Bodybuilder muscles developed through exercise are acquired traits and are not inherited by children.
key_points: Only genetic traits pass to offspring
